text.title: ASP.NET Core Patterns Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules grounding the ASP.NET Core Patterns methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale. Hard rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Pass CancellationToken as the last parameter on every async controller action and thread it through service and repository layers down to the EF Core call.
rule.rationale: Without CancellationToken plumbing, client disconnects and request timeouts do not cancel in-flight DB queries, causing thread-pool starvation under load.

rule.statement: Use [Authorize] and [ProducesResponseType] on every action; configure RequireAuthenticatedUser() globally in Program.cs and opt out per route with [AllowAnonymous].
rule.rationale: Agents copy public endpoints into private controllers and forget [Authorize]; a default-authenticated policy prevents accidental exposure.

rule.statement: Add MapperConfiguration.AssertConfigurationIsValid() to a unit test; AutoMapper mapping errors surface only at request time otherwise.
rule.rationale: Agents add properties to DTOs without updating AutoMapper profiles; tests pass at compile time but prod 500s on first request.

rule.statement: All read queries MUST use AsNoTracking(); without it EF Core builds a change-tracker for read-only data and burns memory.
rule.rationale: Default tracking on read queries is a measurable perf regression at scale; AsNoTracking is the explicit opt-in.

rule.statement: Return ProblemDetails (RFC 7807) for all error paths via a centralized exception middleware; ad-hoc error strings are rejected.
rule.rationale: Without a single error contract, integrators reverse-engineer per-endpoint formats and the API becomes uncacheable for client libraries.
